name: Tower of London
brief: A Norman fortress on the north bank of the Thames founded by William the Conqueror, symbol of royal power for nearly a thousand years.
fact: By tradition, the ravens of the Tower must never leave: legend says the Crown will fall if they ever do.

buildStages.title: Norman White Tower
buildStages.description: Around 1078 William the Conqueror raised the central keep in pale Caen stone on a square plan.

buildStages.title: Inner curtain wall
buildStages.description: Henry III enclosed the fortress with the first ring of walls and thirteen towers.

buildStages.title: Outer wall and moat
buildStages.description: Edward I added a second ring of walls and a wide moat fed by the Thames.

buildStages.title: Modern adaptations
buildStages.description: Through the 16th to 19th centuries the interiors were rebuilt as residence, prison and royal mint.

interiorNotes.title: White Tower keep
interiorNotes.description: The Norman keep contains the Chapel of St John and the original royal hall.

interiorNotes.title: Crown Jewels
interiorNotes.description: The Jewel House safeguards the British regalia behind armoured glass.

interiorNotes.title: Royal armoury
interiorNotes.description: Halls of armour, swords and cannon trace the evolution of royal weaponry.

interiorNotes.title: Tower Green
interiorNotes.description: The inner courtyard where high-ranking prisoners were privately executed.

historicFacts.description: Construction of the White Tower begins under William the Conqueror.
The Princes in the Tower disappear within its walls, an unsolved mystery.
Anne Boleyn is executed on Tower Green by order of Henry VIII.
UNESCO inscribes the Tower of London as a World Heritage Site.

name: Tower of Hercules
brief: The only Roman lighthouse still in use as a navigational aid, raised under Emperor Trajan on an Atlantic headland.
fact: It has guided ships for almost two thousand years and was inscribed by UNESCO in 2009 for that uninterrupted service.

buildStages.title: Roman core
buildStages.description: Around AD 100 the architect Gaius Sevius Lupus built a square three-tier ashlar core.

buildStages.title: External spiral ramp
buildStages.description: An outer ramp let workers carry firewood and oil up to the burning beacon at the top.

buildStages.title: Medieval repairs
buildStages.description: Localised reinforcements through the Middle Ages kept the navigational fire alive.

buildStages.title: Neoclassical refit of 1791
buildStages.description: Engineer Eustaquio Giannini wrapped the Roman core in new ashlar without disturbing the original fabric.

interiorNotes.title: Visible Roman core
interiorNotes.description: Inside, original 2nd century walls and vaults survive intact.

interiorNotes.title: Modern stair
interiorNotes.description: A steel stair climbs through the core to the lantern, alongside the remains of the old ramp.

interiorNotes.title: Interpretation hall
interiorNotes.description: Panels explain how the Roman lighthouse worked and how it evolved.

interiorNotes.title: Active lantern
interiorNotes.description: The lighthouse still flashes for ships entering the port of A Coruña.

historicFacts.description: Built under Emperor Trajan, attested by an inscription dedicated to Mars.
Neoclassical restoration encloses the Roman core without destroying it.
Declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site as a working Roman lighthouse.
It rises 55 metres tall on a 57 metre cliff above the Atlantic.

name: La Giralda
brief: Once the minaret of Seville's Almohad great mosque, later turned into the bell tower of the Christian cathedral.
fact: Instead of stairs, the muezzin could ride a horse all the way to the top thanks to a long internal ramp.

buildStages.title: Almohad foundations
buildStages.description: In 1184 Caliph Abu Yaqub Yusuf orders the minaret; foundations reuse Visigothic and Roman stones.

buildStages.title: Decorated brick shaft
buildStages.description: Brick panels in sebka pattern and twin arched windows decorate every face.

buildStages.title: Renaissance bell stage
buildStages.description: Hernán Ruiz II adds the Christian belfry, lantern and weather vane in 1568.

buildStages.title: El Giraldillo
buildStages.description: A bronze figure of Faith crowns the tower and rotates with the wind, giving the tower its name.

interiorNotes.title: Ramps instead of stairs
interiorNotes.description: Thirty-five ramp sections allow a comfortable climb to the bell stage.

interiorNotes.title: Bell chamber
interiorNotes.description: Twenty-four bronze bells, each with its own name, mark the hours and feast days.

interiorNotes.title: Viewpoint over Seville
interiorNotes.description: From the top the historic centre, the Alcazar and the Guadalquivir spread out below.

interiorNotes.title: Original minaret traces
interiorNotes.description: The base shows the Almohad walls and brick decoration of the original minaret.

historicFacts.description: Construction begins as the minaret of the Almohad Friday mosque.
Following the Christian conquest of Seville the tower is reused as a Christian belfry.
The bell stage is finished and the Giraldillo weathervane is set in place.
It stands 96 metres tall and was for centuries the tallest tower in Europe.

name: Belém Tower
brief: A Manueline bastion at the mouth of the Tagus, built to defend Lisbon's harbour and to serve as a ceremonial gate for the explorers.
fact: It originally stood in the middle of the river; centuries of silting brought the bank gradually up to the tower.

buildStages.title: Artillery platform
buildStages.description: Between 1514 and 1519 a hexagonal bastion with cannon ports is built at water level.

buildStages.title: Manueline tower
buildStages.description: A four-storey tower rises above the bastion with balconies, turrets and maritime carving.

buildStages.title: Manueline decoration
buildStages.description: Stone ropes, armillary spheres and Crosses of the Order of Christ adorn the cornices and windows.

buildStages.title: 19th century restoration
buildStages.description: After serving as prison and customs post the tower is restored to recover its original Manueline look.

interiorNotes.title: Governor's hall
interiorNotes.description: Stone-fireplace room with windows overlooking the estuary.

interiorNotes.title: King's hall
interiorNotes.description: Gothic vaulted hall where the garrison's small arms were stored.

interiorNotes.title: Chapel
interiorNotes.description: A small chapel with a ribbed vault decorated with Manueline motifs.

interiorNotes.title: Battlement terrace
interiorNotes.description: Open terrace with views of the river and the nearby Jerónimos Monastery.

historicFacts.description: King Manuel I commissions the tower from Francisco de Arruda to defend the Tagus.
After the Iberian Union the tower serves as a military post and later a political prison.
Restoration begins to recover Manueline elements lost over the centuries.
Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site together with the Jerónimos Monastery.

name: Torre del Oro
brief: A twelve-sided albarrana tower on the Guadalquivir, built by the Almohads to control entry into the river port of Seville.
fact: Its name may come from the golden reflection of glazed tiles that once covered its dome.

buildStages.title: River foundations
buildStages.description: Around 1220 the dodecagonal first body was raised on stone and lime to resist the Guadalquivir floods.

buildStages.title: Almohad main body
buildStages.description: Rammed earth walls reinforced with ashlar form twelve identical sides.

buildStages.title: Christian second body
buildStages.description: King Pedro I added the hexagonal middle body in the 14th century, also on a polygonal plan.

buildStages.title: 18th century lantern
buildStages.description: Sebastián Van der Borcht crowned the tower with a cylindrical body and dome after the 1755 Lisbon earthquake.

interiorNotes.title: Vaulted ground floor
interiorNotes.description: Stone ribbed vault that served as a store room and to control the river chain.

interiorNotes.title: Spiral stair
interiorNotes.description: A narrow stair within the wall climbs through all three bodies to the lantern.

interiorNotes.title: Naval museum
interiorNotes.description: Today it houses ship models, navigation instruments and engravings on Seville's maritime history.

interiorNotes.title: Upper viewpoint
interiorNotes.description: From the dome the river and the nearby Maestranza bullring are visible.

historicFacts.description: Almohad governor Abu l-Ulà orders its construction to defend the river port.
After the Christian conquest the tower joins the military precinct of the Castilian fleet.
The Lisbon earthquake damages the tower, which is later crowned with the present Baroque lantern.
The naval museum opens inside the tower and remains active today.

name: Leaning Tower of Pisa
brief: Free-standing campanile of Pisa Cathedral, famous for the dramatic tilt caused by the soft soil beneath its foundations.
fact: It started leaning during construction, forcing the upper storeys to be built with a deliberate counter-curve.

buildStages.title: First storey (1173)
buildStages.description: Work begins with a solid white marble base and blind arcades.

buildStages.title: Pause for the lean
buildStages.description: After three storeys construction halts for nearly a century when the tilt becomes visible.

buildStages.title: Compensating curve
buildStages.description: Giovanni di Simone resumes the work, slightly bending the upper floors to offset the tilt.

buildStages.title: Belfry crown
buildStages.description: In 1372 Tommaso di Andrea Pisano completes the tower with the bell chamber.

interiorNotes.title: Spiral stair
interiorNotes.description: 294 marble steps wind through the wall to the upper galleries.

interiorNotes.title: Open galleries
interiorNotes.description: Arched galleries on each storey lighten the load on the foundations.

interiorNotes.title: Bell chamber
interiorNotes.description: Seven bells tuned to the medieval musical modes hang at the top.

interiorNotes.title: Views over the Square of Miracles
interiorNotes.description: From the top, the cathedral and baptistery spread below.

historicFacts.description: Bonanno Pisano begins work on the campanile.
Construction is finally completed with the bell chamber after almost two centuries.
The tower is closed to the public to begin major stabilisation works.
It reopens after engineers correct part of the tilt without removing it altogether.

name: Big Ben
brief: The clock tower of the Palace of Westminster, seat of the British Parliament and a landmark of Victorian Gothic Revival.
fact: Big Ben is actually the name of the great bell; the tower itself has been officially called the Elizabeth Tower since 2012.

buildStages.title: Gothic Revival design
buildStages.description: Charles Barry and Augustus Pugin design the tower after the 1834 fire that destroyed the old palace.

buildStages.title: Iron skeleton
buildStages.description: A wrought-iron skeleton is built and clad in Anston limestone.

buildStages.title: Clock mechanism
buildStages.description: Edmund Beckett Denison and Edward Dent design the great clock, installed in 1859.

buildStages.title: 2017–2022 restoration
buildStages.description: Five years of works restore gilding, dials and mechanism to their original state.

interiorNotes.title: Mechanism room
interiorNotes.description: Brass and iron gears whose speed is fine-tuned by adding or removing pennies on the pendulum.

interiorNotes.title: Bell chamber
interiorNotes.description: Five bells, including Big Ben itself which weighs over 13 tonnes.

interiorNotes.title: Clock dials
interiorNotes.description: Four opaline dials lit from behind through Birmingham glass panels.

interiorNotes.title: Maintenance walkway
interiorNotes.description: A walkway gives access to the dials for cleaning and repairs.

historicFacts.description: The clock first chimes from the new tower.
The clock chamber survives Nazi bombing while parts of Parliament collapse.
The tower is renamed Elizabeth Tower in honour of the Queen's Jubilee.
Public visits resume after the major restoration.

name: Eiffel Tower
brief: A wrought-iron lattice tower built for the 1889 World's Fair, becoming an icon of late nineteenth century engineering.
fact: It was meant to be dismantled after twenty years but was saved when a military telegraphy antenna was installed at the top.

buildStages.title: Four foundation pillars
buildStages.description: Each pillar rests on a concrete block with inclined iron feet to absorb the load.

buildStages.title: First platform
buildStages.description: The first level was completed in March 1888 using steam-powered cranes.

buildStages.title: Second platform and arch
buildStages.description: The four legs join into an iron arch that supports the second platform.

buildStages.title: Summit and lantern
buildStages.description: The final section is bolted in place piece by piece, reaching 312 metres, the world record in 1889.

interiorNotes.title: Eastern pillar
interiorNotes.description: Holds the original Otis hydraulic lifts now updated with modern controls.

interiorNotes.title: Jules Verne restaurant
interiorNotes.description: Panoramic restaurant on the second platform with views over Paris.

interiorNotes.title: Eiffel's office
interiorNotes.description: At the top, Gustave Eiffel's private office where he hosted distinguished guests is preserved.

interiorNotes.title: Radio antennas
interiorNotes.description: Television and radio transmission equipment crown the structure.

historicFacts.description: Inaugurated for the World's Fair celebrating the centenary of the French Revolution.
A military antenna saves the tower from its scheduled demolition.
Television antennas add 24 metres to its height.
It welcomes around 7 million visitors a year.

name: Galata Tower
brief: A cylindrical stone tower built by the Genoese in the Pera quarter to watch over the Golden Horn.
fact: Legend has it that in the 17th century Hezârfen Ahmed Çelebi flew from the tower with home-made wings across the Bosphorus.

buildStages.title: Byzantine base
buildStages.description: An older Byzantine fortlet provided the foundation for the Genoese tower.

buildStages.title: Genoese stone shaft
buildStages.description: Around 1348 the Genoese raised the main stone tower to defend their colony.

buildStages.title: Ottoman adaptation
buildStages.description: After 1453 the Ottomans used it as a watchtower and a prison.

buildStages.title: Conical roof
buildStages.description: In the 19th century the conical lead roof that defines its silhouette today was added.

interiorNotes.title: Modern spiral stair
interiorNotes.description: A renewed staircase gives access to the upper floors alongside a lift.

interiorNotes.title: Historic café
interiorNotes.description: Intermediate floors host a café and exhibition spaces.

interiorNotes.title: Panoramic gallery
interiorNotes.description: From the top gallery the Bosphorus, Hagia Sophia and the Golden Horn unfold in every direction.

interiorNotes.title: Exhibition hall
interiorNotes.description: Models and panels explain the Genoese and Ottoman history of the neighbourhood.

historicFacts.description: The Genoese build it as Christea Turris, the Tower of Christ.
After the conquest of Constantinople the tower passes to the Ottoman Empire.
Hezârfen's legendary flight from the tower across the Bosphorus.
The tower reopens after a full restoration of body and roof.

name: Hassan Tower
brief: Unfinished minaret of an Almohad mosque that Sultan Yacub al-Mansur intended as the largest in the Islamic world.
fact: It was meant to reach 86 metres tall, but the death of the sultan in 1199 left the work permanently halted.

buildStages.title: Layout of the precinct
buildStages.description: Around 1191 a mosque of 183 by 139 metres with 348 columns was planned.

buildStages.title: Foundations of the minaret
buildStages.description: The minaret rose in red sandstone ashlar, similar in style to the Marrakech minarets.

buildStages.title: Sebka decoration
buildStages.description: Each face of the minaret carries panels of sebka and polylobed arches.

buildStages.title: Interrupted work
buildStages.description: When Sultan Yacub al-Mansur died in 1199 work stopped at the present 44 metres.

interiorNotes.title: Internal ramp
interiorNotes.description: Like other Almohad towers, an internal ramp let one ride up on horseback.

interiorNotes.title: Vaulted halls
interiorNotes.description: Internal halls preserve their original stone groin vaults.

interiorNotes.title: Forest of column stumps
interiorNotes.description: Beside the tower stand the truncated columns intended for the prayer hall.

interiorNotes.title: Nearby mausoleum
interiorNotes.description: Facing the tower, the modern Mausoleum of Mohammed V completes the monumental ensemble.

historicFacts.description: Almohad Sultan Yacub al-Mansur orders its construction.
The sultan dies and work stops for good.
The Lisbon earthquake topples many columns but the tower itself stands.
UNESCO inscribes the site as part of the World Heritage of Rabat.

name: Lighthouse of Alexandria
brief: One of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, raised on the island of Pharos to guide sailors into the harbour of Alexandria.
fact: Its name, Pharos, became the word for lighthouse in many European languages.

buildStages.title: Hellenistic design
buildStages.description: Sostratus of Cnidus designed the lighthouse under Ptolemy I and II, around 280 BC.

buildStages.title: Square base
buildStages.description: A square base roughly 30 metres tall sat on the island of Pharos.

buildStages.title: Octagonal tier
buildStages.description: A second octagonal tier rose above the square base.

buildStages.title: Cylindrical lantern
buildStages.description: The top cylindrical level held a fire and, according to ancient writers, mirrors that boosted its light.

interiorNotes.title: Service ramps
interiorNotes.description: Internal ramps let workers carry fuel up to the burning beacon.

interiorNotes.title: Keepers' quarters
interiorNotes.description: Rooms across the levels housed the garrison and the lighthouse staff.

interiorNotes.title: Lantern hall
interiorNotes.description: Crowned, according to ancient sources, by a colossal statue, possibly of Zeus or Poseidon.

interiorNotes.title: Bronze reflectors
interiorNotes.description: Arab sources describe polished mirrors that increased the light's range over the sea.

historicFacts.description: Inaugurated under Ptolemy II of Egypt.
A major earthquake causes the first severe damage.
Another earthquake brings down most of the structure.
Its remains are reused to build the Qaitbay fortress on the same island.

name: El Salvador Tower
brief: The Mudejar bell tower of the church of El Salvador in Teruel, a leading example of Aragonese Mudejar art in brick and ceramic.
fact: Its outer skin of green and white glazed tiles makes it almost a twin of the nearby Tower of San Martín.

buildStages.title: Pass-through base
buildStages.description: The tower rises over an arched passage so as not to block the street, a hallmark of Teruel Mudejar.

buildStages.title: Concentric inner tower
buildStages.description: Inside there is a second concentric tower, with the staircase running between the two shells.

buildStages.title: Ceramic decoration
buildStages.description: Panels of glazed ceramic with geometric motifs cover the entire exterior.

buildStages.title: Bell stage
buildStages.description: Crowned by a stage of round-arched openings where the bells hang.

interiorNotes.title: Double concentric tower
interiorNotes.description: An internal layout that recalls the Kutubia Mosque minaret in Marrakech.

interiorNotes.title: Stair between the walls
interiorNotes.description: A narrow stair climbs in the gap between the outer and inner shells.

interiorNotes.title: Exhibition space
interiorNotes.description: Museum spaces explain the Islamic influence on Aragonese Mudejar art.

interiorNotes.title: Viewpoint over Teruel
interiorNotes.description: From the top, the old town and the other Mudejar monuments are visible.

historicFacts.description: Construction begins under Christian rule of the Kingdom of Aragon.
UNESCO inscribes the Mudejar of Teruel as a World Heritage Site.
The inscription is later expanded to cover all Aragonese Mudejar.
It rises about 40 metres and closely resembles the Tower of San Martín.

name: Prague Astronomical Clock Tower
brief: Tower of the Old Town Hall in Prague, famous for an astronomical clock that displays the motion of the sun and moon.
fact: Every hour the windows above the dial open and the twelve apostles parade in front of locals and tourists.

buildStages.title: Gothic construction
buildStages.description: Around 1338 the tower rises as the seat of the Old Town municipal council.

buildStages.title: Astronomical clock (1410)
buildStages.description: Clockmaker Mikulás of Kadan and mathematician Jan Sindel design the original astronomical clock.

buildStages.title: Manes calendar (1866)
buildStages.description: Painter Josef Manes creates the second dial with scenes of the months.

buildStages.title: Restoration after 1945
buildStages.description: After damage in the Second World War the clock is repaired and the figures renewed.

interiorNotes.title: Gothic council hall
interiorNotes.description: Star-vaulted hall in the former plenary chamber of the Old Town council.

interiorNotes.title: Mechanism of the Orloj
interiorNotes.description: Gears and zodiacal discs that drive the clock and the figures of the apostles.

interiorNotes.title: Gothic chapel
interiorNotes.description: Chapel with restored stained glass inside the main body of the tower.

interiorNotes.title: Viewpoint over the square
interiorNotes.description: From the upper gallery the entire Old Town Square unfolds below.

historicFacts.description: The astronomical clock is built, considered the oldest still working in the world.
The circular calendar and moving figures are added.
The tower suffers serious damage at the end of the Second World War.
After a major restoration its mechanism and historic appearance are recovered.

name: Coca Castle
brief: A Mudejar brick castle raised by the Fonseca family in the Segovian town of Coca, considered a masterpiece of late Hispanic Gothic.
fact: It barely had any military use: it was built more as a palatial fortress to display the wealth of Archbishop Alonso de Fonseca.

buildStages.title: Moat and outer barrier
buildStages.description: A wide dry moat is dug and an outer barrier with pentagonal towers is raised.

buildStages.title: Main precinct
buildStages.description: Around 1473 Mudejar masters raise the main body with four corner towers.

buildStages.title: Keep tower
buildStages.description: The square keep rises above the rest of the precinct in the northern corner.

buildStages.title: Mudejar decoration
buildStages.description: Bands of brick, sebka and lozenges cover every surface with geometric motifs.

interiorNotes.title: Inner courtyard
interiorNotes.description: Central courtyard with double-height galleries and lowered arches.

interiorNotes.title: Chapel
interiorNotes.description: Rib vault from the 15th century with Mudejar plasterwork.

interiorNotes.title: Noble halls
interiorNotes.description: Rooms with carved wooden ceilings made for the Fonseca lineage.

interiorNotes.title: Dungeons
interiorNotes.description: Small cellar cells served as prison and storage.

historicFacts.description: Construction begins by order of Archbishop Alonso de Fonseca.
The castle suffers damage during the War of the Castilian Communities.
It becomes the seat of a State forestry school.
It is listed as a Cultural Asset and a National Monument.

name: Tavira Tower
brief: Lookout tower of the former palace of the Marquises of Recaño, officially appointed as the watchtower of the port of Cádiz.
fact: Inside it works the only active camera obscura in Spain, which projects the city in real time onto a concave screen.

buildStages.title: Recaño palace
buildStages.description: The palace is built in the 18th century with the tower as its tallest element.

buildStages.title: Merchants' lookout
buildStages.description: The towers of Cádiz let merchants watch ships arriving in the harbour.

buildStages.title: Royal designation (1778)
buildStages.description: King Charles III names it the official watchtower of the port as the highest in the old town.

buildStages.title: Camera obscura (1994)
buildStages.description: A camera obscura is installed under the dome, projecting the city through mirrors and lenses.

interiorNotes.title: Camera obscura room
interiorNotes.description: A darkened room projects the live image of the city onto a concave white surface.

interiorNotes.title: Exhibition halls
interiorNotes.description: Two floors trace the history of the watchtowers of Cádiz.

interiorNotes.title: Upper terrace
interiorNotes.description: Lookout 45 metres above sea level with views of the entire city and the bay.

interiorNotes.title: Model of Cádiz
interiorNotes.description: A scale model shows the location of the original 126 watchtowers of the city.

historicFacts.description: King Charles III names it the official watchtower of the port of Cádiz.
Over 160 watchtowers are recorded in the old town of Cádiz.
The camera obscura is inaugurated, the first of its kind in Spain.
The tower reaches 45 metres above sea level and dominates the entire city.

name: Himeji Castle
brief: Hilltop Japanese castle nicknamed the White Heron for its brilliant white plaster, the most visited and best preserved feudal castle in Japan.
fact: The complex contains 83 wooden buildings and survived the firebombing of Himeji in 1945 untouched.

buildStages.title: Akamatsu fortress (1333)
buildStages.description: Akamatsu Norimura builds the first fortifications on Himeyama hill.

buildStages.title: Toyotomi expansion (1581)
buildStages.description: Toyotomi Hideyoshi raises a three-storey keep on the existing fort.

buildStages.title: Ikeda main keep (1609)
buildStages.description: Ikeda Terumasa rebuilds the castle on its present grand scale with the six-storey main keep.

buildStages.title: Heisei restoration (2009-2015)
buildStages.description: A six-year restoration replaces roof tiles and white plaster, restoring the heron-white look.

interiorNotes.title: Main keep stairs
interiorNotes.description: Six narrow wooden floors connected by steep ladders climb to a small shrine at the top.

interiorNotes.title: Stone-throwing platforms
interiorNotes.description: Hidden balconies above gates allowed defenders to drop stones on attackers.

interiorNotes.title: Princess Sen's quarters
interiorNotes.description: Wooden private apartments connected to the West Bailey by a long corridor.

interiorNotes.title: Loophole grid
interiorNotes.description: Hundreds of triangular and circular loopholes pierce the walls for archers and gunners.

historicFacts.description: Akamatsu Norimura builds the first fortification on Himeyama hill.
Ikeda Terumasa completes the great keep that survives today.
Inscribed as one of Japan's first UNESCO World Heritage Sites.
Reopens after the Heisei era restoration.

name: Edinburgh Castle
brief: Royal fortress on the volcanic Castle Rock above central Edinburgh, occupied since the Iron Age and a key seat of the Scottish crown.
fact: It is one of the most besieged places in Britain — at least 26 documented sieges have battered its walls.

buildStages.title: Iron Age fort
buildStages.description: Earliest occupation on Castle Rock dates to the 2nd century AD.

buildStages.title: St Margaret's Chapel (1130)
buildStages.description: King David I builds the small Romanesque chapel that is the oldest surviving building in Edinburgh.

buildStages.title: David's Tower (1370s)
buildStages.description: King David II raises the tall L-shaped keep that dominates the rock.

buildStages.title: Half-Moon Battery (1573)
buildStages.description: After the Lang Siege a curved artillery battery is built around the ruined David's Tower.

interiorNotes.title: Crown Room
interiorNotes.description: Houses the Honours of Scotland — the crown, sceptre and sword of state.

interiorNotes.title: Great Hall
interiorNotes.description: Hammer-beam roof from 1511 over the medieval banquet hall.

interiorNotes.title: St Margaret's Chapel
interiorNotes.description: Tiny Romanesque chapel of 1130, the oldest building in Edinburgh.

interiorNotes.title: Mons Meg
interiorNotes.description: Six-tonne medieval bombard cannon presented to James II in 1457.

historicFacts.description: St Margaret's Chapel is built — the oldest surviving structure.
After the Lang Siege the castle is largely rebuilt as a military fortress.
Walter Scott rediscovers the Honours of Scotland walled up in a chest.
Around 2 million visitors per year make it Scotland's most popular paid attraction.

name: Krak des Chevaliers
brief: Concentric Crusader fortress in western Syria, considered one of the most important preserved medieval castles in the world.
fact: T. E. Lawrence called it 'the finest castle in the world, certainly the most picturesque'.

buildStages.title: Kurdish fortress (1031)
buildStages.description: The Emir of Aleppo builds a small mountain fortress on the site.

buildStages.title: Hospitaller stronghold (1142)
buildStages.description: The Knights Hospitaller take over and rebuild the castle on a grand scale.

buildStages.title: Concentric defences (1170s)
buildStages.description: After earthquakes the fortress is rebuilt with two concentric rings of curtain walls.

buildStages.title: Outer glacis (13th c.)
buildStages.description: Massive sloped stone glacis is added around the inner walls to deflect siege weapons.

interiorNotes.title: Inner ward halls
interiorNotes.description: Vaulted Gothic halls with a chapel converted into a mosque after 1271.

interiorNotes.title: Loggia of Knights
interiorNotes.description: Elegant arcaded loggia overlooking the inner courtyard.

interiorNotes.title: Great Hall
interiorNotes.description: Long ribbed-vault hall used for chapter meetings and feasts.

interiorNotes.title: Storerooms
interiorNotes.description: Massive vaulted storerooms could feed 2000 men for five years of siege.

historicFacts.description: The Knights Hospitaller take over the castle and begin the great rebuild.
Sultan Baibars finally takes the castle by surrender after a brief siege.
Inscribed by UNESCO as a World Heritage Site.
Suffers some damage during the Syrian civil war but main structure survives.

name: Round Tower
brief: Cylindrical 17th-century tower built by King Christian IV as an astronomical observatory, the oldest functioning observatory in Europe.
fact: Inside, a 209-metre helical ramp climbs to the top — Tsar Peter the Great rode his horse up it in 1716.

buildStages.title: Royal commission (1637)
buildStages.description: Christian IV commissions a tower attached to Trinitatis Church for astronomy.

buildStages.title: Cylindrical brick shaft
buildStages.description: The cylindrical brick shell rises with a wide internal helical ramp instead of stairs.

buildStages.title: Observation platform (1642)
buildStages.description: The tower is inaugurated with an open viewing platform on top.

buildStages.title: Modern observatory dome
buildStages.description: A small dome is added in the 19th century to house a refractor telescope.

interiorNotes.title: Helical ramp
interiorNotes.description: A 209 metre brick ramp spirals seven and a half times to the top.

interiorNotes.title: Library hall
interiorNotes.description: A vaulted hall above the church served as the university library.

interiorNotes.title: Glass floor
interiorNotes.description: A modern transparent floor lets visitors look down 25 metres into the church loft.

interiorNotes.title: Viewing platform
interiorNotes.description: Open platform with views over the rooftops of central Copenhagen.

historicFacts.description: Inaugurated by Christian IV as an astronomical observatory.
Tsar Peter the Great famously rides his horse up the spiral ramp.
The university observatory moves out, the platform stays open to the public.
Still the oldest working observatory in Europe.

name: St Mark's Campanile
brief: Brick bell tower of St Mark's Basilica on Piazza San Marco, the tallest structure in Venice and a long-standing landmark of the lagoon.
fact: The original tower collapsed without warning on 14 July 1902; the current one was rebuilt to look exactly the same and reopened in 1912.

buildStages.title: Watchtower foundations (9th c.)
buildStages.description: Begun in the 9th century as a lighthouse and watchtower for the lagoon.

buildStages.title: Brick shaft (12th c.)
buildStages.description: The brick shaft reaches its present height by the 1100s.

buildStages.title: Loggetta (1549)
buildStages.description: Jacopo Sansovino adds the marble loggetta at the base.

buildStages.title: Reconstruction (1902-1912)
buildStages.description: After the collapse the tower is rebuilt identically with reinforced internal staircases.

interiorNotes.title: Internal ramp
interiorNotes.description: Until 1902 a gentle internal ramp ran up the shaft; today there is a lift to the belfry.

interiorNotes.title: Bell chamber
interiorNotes.description: Five bronze bells hang in the white stone belfry, each with its historical name.

interiorNotes.title: Loggetta
interiorNotes.description: Sansovino's small Renaissance loggetta with bronze figures of pagan gods.

interiorNotes.title: Viewpoint over the lagoon
interiorNotes.description: The terrace gives views over the lagoon to the Dolomites on a clear day.

historicFacts.description: The brick shaft reaches its full height.
Galileo Galilei demonstrates his telescope to the Doge from the belfry.
The medieval tower collapses without warning; no one is killed.
An identical reconstruction is inaugurated for the 1000th anniversary of the original.

name: Giotto's Campanile
brief: Free-standing Gothic bell tower of Florence Cathedral designed by the painter Giotto, clad in white, pink and green marble.
fact: Giotto only lived to see the lowest level finished — the rest was completed by Andrea Pisano and Francesco Talenti.

buildStages.title: Giotto's first level (1334)
buildStages.description: Master painter Giotto designs the campanile and supervises the lowest hexagonal panels.

buildStages.title: Pisano (1337-1348)
buildStages.description: After Giotto's death Andrea Pisano continues the second register and the niches.

buildStages.title: Talenti's upper levels
buildStages.description: Francesco Talenti completes the upper stories and the projecting top platform.

buildStages.title: Final marble cladding (1359)
buildStages.description: The complete polychrome marble cladding is finished, with no spire as originally planned.

interiorNotes.title: Stone stair
interiorNotes.description: 414 steps wind through the wall to the open viewing terrace at the top.

interiorNotes.title: Reliefs of human work
interiorNotes.description: The lowest hexagonal panels show the trades and arts of mankind.

interiorNotes.title: Niches with prophets
interiorNotes.description: Donatello carved several of the prophets in the second-storey niches.

interiorNotes.title: Top viewpoint
interiorNotes.description: Open terrace with views of Brunelleschi's dome at eye level.

historicFacts.description: Giotto begins the design as official architect of Florence.
Talenti finishes the campanile without the originally planned spire.
The original sculpted panels are moved to the cathedral museum, replaced by copies.
Inscribed as part of the Historic Centre of Florence UNESCO site.

name: Spasskaya Tower
brief: Main gateway tower of the Moscow Kremlin facing Red Square, crowned by the great Kremlin Clock and a glowing red ruby star.
fact: The chimes of the Kremlin Clock are the official sound of the Russian New Year, broadcast every 31 December.

buildStages.title: Italian gate tower (1491)
buildStages.description: Pietro Antonio Solari builds the brick gate tower as part of Ivan III's Kremlin walls.

buildStages.title: English clock (1626)
buildStages.description: Christopher Galloway installs the first English-made clock in the tower.

buildStages.title: Multi-tier spire (1625)
buildStages.description: The Russian-style tented spire is added over the original Italian gate tower.

buildStages.title: Ruby star (1937)
buildStages.description: A glowing five-pointed ruby star replaces the imperial double-headed eagle.

interiorNotes.title: Gate of the Saviour
interiorNotes.description: Through the iconic of Christ on the outer side, the main ceremonial entrance to the Kremlin.

interiorNotes.title: Clock mechanism room
interiorNotes.description: Houses the great mechanism that drives the four clock faces and the chimes.

interiorNotes.title: Bell chamber
interiorNotes.description: Nine bronze bells play the Russian national anthem every three hours.

interiorNotes.title: Watchman's gallery
interiorNotes.description: Wooden gallery used by the original gate guards.

historicFacts.description: Built by Pietro Antonio Solari as the main gate to the Kremlin.
The current clock mechanism by the Butenop brothers is installed.
The ruby star replaces the imperial eagle on the spire.
The bells are restored to chime the Russian national anthem.

name: Qutb Minar
brief: Tallest brick minaret in the world, begun in 1192 by Qutb-ud-din Aibak as a tower of victory next to the first mosque of Delhi.
fact: The five tapering tiers were each built by a different sultan over more than 150 years.

buildStages.title: Aibak's first tier (1192)
buildStages.description: Qutb-ud-din Aibak begins the minaret as a tower of victory next to the Quwwat-ul-Islam mosque.

buildStages.title: Iltutmish's expansion (1220)
buildStages.description: Sultan Iltutmish completes three more tiers, raising the tower to about 70 metres.

buildStages.title: Tughluq replacement (1369)
buildStages.description: After lightning damage Firuz Shah Tughluq rebuilds the top two tiers in marble and red sandstone.

buildStages.title: Smith's repairs (1828)
buildStages.description: British army engineer Robert Smith repairs the top tier, adding an unsuccessful Bengali-style cupola later removed.

interiorNotes.title: Spiral stair
interiorNotes.description: 379 stone steps spiral inside the minaret to the projecting balconies.

interiorNotes.title: Carved bands
interiorNotes.description: Bands of Quranic calligraphy and decorative motifs run around each tier.

interiorNotes.title: Iron Pillar nearby
interiorNotes.description: The 4th-century Iron Pillar in the courtyard has not rusted in over 1500 years.

interiorNotes.title: Mosque ruins
interiorNotes.description: Surrounded by the ruins of the Quwwat-ul-Islam mosque, the first mosque built in Delhi.

historicFacts.description: Qutb-ud-din Aibak begins construction after his conquest of Delhi.
Lightning destroys the upper tiers; Firuz Shah Tughluq rebuilds them.
Closed to internal access after a stairwell stampede kills 45 people.
Inscribed by UNESCO as a World Heritage Site.

name: Kalyan Minaret
brief: Tall brick minaret of the Po-i-Kalyan complex in Bukhara, considered one of the masterpieces of medieval Central Asian architecture.
fact: Genghis Khan ordered Bukhara destroyed in 1220 but spared the minaret because he was so impressed by its beauty.

buildStages.title: Karakhanid foundation (1127)
buildStages.description: Arslan Khan begins the minaret next to the Kalyan Mosque.

buildStages.title: Tapered brick shaft
buildStages.description: The shaft tapers from a 9 metre wide base to about 6 metres at the top, reaching 47 metres.

buildStages.title: Decorative belts
buildStages.description: Twelve horizontal belts of geometric brick patterns wrap the shaft, each different.

buildStages.title: Lantern (12th c.)
buildStages.description: An open arched lantern of sixteen windows is added on top with a small dome.

interiorNotes.title: Spiral brick stair
interiorNotes.description: 104 brick steps spiral inside the shaft to the lantern.

interiorNotes.title: Glazed inscription band
interiorNotes.description: A turquoise glazed band near the top names the patron Arslan Khan.

interiorNotes.title: Lantern
interiorNotes.description: Open belvedere with sixteen arched windows giving views over the Po-i-Kalyan square.

interiorNotes.title: Connecting bridge
interiorNotes.description: A small covered bridge once linked the minaret to the roof of the mosque.

historicFacts.description: Arslan Khan completes the minaret after his earlier wooden tower collapsed.
Genghis Khan reportedly spares the minaret after sacking Bukhara.
Soviet authorities prohibit the call to prayer from the minaret.
Inscribed by UNESCO as part of the Historic Centre of Bukhara.

name: Cordouan Lighthouse
brief: Renaissance lighthouse on a rocky platform at the mouth of the Gironde estuary, often called the Versailles of the Sea.
fact: Inside the second floor is a small chapel with a marble altar — sailors could attend Mass while waiting out storms.

buildStages.title: Black Prince beacon (14th c.)
buildStages.description: An octagonal beacon by Edward the Black Prince once stood on the same shoal.

buildStages.title: Renaissance tower (1584-1611)
buildStages.description: Architect Louis de Foix builds the present ornate tower for Henri III and Henri IV.

buildStages.title: Royal additions (17th c.)
buildStages.description: King's apartment and chapel are decorated with carved pilasters and statues.

buildStages.title: Top tier raised (1789)
buildStages.description: Engineer Joseph Teulère rebuilds the upper tiers, adding 30 metres to the original height.

interiorNotes.title: King's apartment
interiorNotes.description: Reserved for royal visits, decorated with carved pilasters and a Latin inscription.

interiorNotes.title: Chapel
interiorNotes.description: Small marble-altared chapel where Mass could be said for visiting sailors.

interiorNotes.title: Spiral stair
interiorNotes.description: 301 stone steps wind to the lantern through alternating tiers.

interiorNotes.title: Active lantern
interiorNotes.description: The lighthouse still flashes at night, automated since 2006.

historicFacts.description: Inaugurated as the most ornate lighthouse in Europe.
Teulère raises the tower to its present 67 metres.
Listed among the first French historic monuments.
Inscribed by UNESCO as a World Heritage Site.

name: Bran Castle
brief: Medieval castle on a rocky cliff in Transylvania, popularly associated with Bram Stoker's Dracula.
fact: Stoker probably never saw the castle — its 'Dracula' connection was invented by 20th-century tourism.

buildStages.title: Wooden Teutonic fort (1212)
buildStages.description: The Teutonic Knights build a wooden border fort on the rock.

buildStages.title: Saxon stone castle (1377)
buildStages.description: The Saxons of Brașov build the present stone castle as a customs station.

buildStages.title: Hungarian governorship (15th c.)
buildStages.description: Used as a Hungarian frontier fortress against the Ottoman Empire.

buildStages.title: Royal residence (1920)
buildStages.description: Donated to Queen Marie of Romania who turns it into a private summer residence.

interiorNotes.title: Inner courtyard
interiorNotes.description: Tiny irregular courtyard surrounded by tall white walls.

interiorNotes.title: Queen Marie's apartments
interiorNotes.description: Restored 1920s royal apartments with embroidered fabrics and carved Romanian furniture.

interiorNotes.title: Secret stair
interiorNotes.description: A narrow hidden stair connects two floors through a fireplace.

interiorNotes.title: Torture chamber exhibit
interiorNotes.description: Modern installation with replicas of medieval interrogation tools.

historicFacts.description: The Saxons of Brașov complete the present stone castle.
Donated by the city of Brașov to Queen Marie of Romania.
Confiscated by the communist regime; the royal collection is dispersed.
Returned to the descendants of Queen Marie and reopened as a private museum.

name: Great Mosque Spiral Minaret
brief: Helical spiral minaret of the Great Mosque of Samarra, an Abbasid masterpiece nicknamed the Malwiya.
fact: The 52-metre cone is climbed by an external ramp wrapping five times around the outside.

buildStages.title: Mosque commission (848)
buildStages.description: Caliph al-Mutawakkil orders the construction of the Great Mosque of Samarra.

buildStages.title: Square base
buildStages.description: A massive 33 metre square brick base is laid for the minaret.

buildStages.title: Helical cone (851)
buildStages.description: The conical tower with its external spiral ramp rises to 52 metres above the desert.

buildStages.title: Truncation (2005)
buildStages.description: An insurgent attack damages the upper section, but the base and most of the spiral survive.

interiorNotes.title: External spiral ramp
interiorNotes.description: The ramp is fully external — no internal stair exists.

interiorNotes.title: Small lantern
interiorNotes.description: A small octagonal lantern sat at the very top, holding the muezzin.

interiorNotes.title: Mosque enclosure
interiorNotes.description: The minaret stands in the largest mosque enclosure in the world by area.

interiorNotes.title: Brick layers
interiorNotes.description: The brick courses can be read like tree rings showing season-by-season construction.

historicFacts.description: Completed under Caliph al-Mutawakkil as part of the Great Mosque.
The mosque is destroyed by the Mongols, but the minaret survives.
An explosion damages the top of the spiral during the Iraq War.
Inscribed by UNESCO as a World Heritage Site (currently endangered).

name: Tower of David
brief: Ancient citadel near the Jaffa Gate of Jerusalem, with foundations dating to the Hasmonean period and a Mamluk minaret often mistaken for King David's tower.
fact: Despite the name, the most prominent tower (the minaret) was actually built by Mamluks in 1655, more than 2500 years after King David.

buildStages.title: Hasmonean walls (2nd c. BC)
buildStages.description: The first fortifications are added to Jerusalem's western wall.

buildStages.title: Herod's Phasael Tower (37 BC)
buildStages.description: Herod the Great builds three massive towers; the base of Phasael survives in the citadel.

buildStages.title: Crusader fortress (12th c.)
buildStages.description: The Crusaders rebuild the citadel as the royal residence of Jerusalem.

buildStages.title: Mamluk minaret (1655)
buildStages.description: The Ottomans add the slender minaret that gives the citadel its modern silhouette.

interiorNotes.title: Phasael base
interiorNotes.description: Huge Herodian ashlar blocks of the original Phasael Tower visible at the base.

interiorNotes.title: Inner courtyard
interiorNotes.description: Open archaeological courtyard exposing layers from many centuries.

interiorNotes.title: Citadel museum
interiorNotes.description: Multimedia exhibits trace the history of Jerusalem through twenty centuries.

interiorNotes.title: Minaret viewpoint
interiorNotes.description: The Mamluk minaret can be climbed for views over the Old City.

historicFacts.description: Herod the Great fortifies the western wall with three great towers.
The Crusaders take Jerusalem and rebuild the citadel as a royal residence.
The Mamluk minaret is added, giving the modern outline of the tower.
Reopened as the Tower of David Museum of the History of Jerusalem.

name: Karlštejn Castle
brief: Gothic castle 30 km south-west of Prague founded by King Charles IV as the safe-keeping of the Imperial Crown Jewels.
fact: The Chapel of the Holy Cross inside the Great Tower is decorated with 130 panel paintings by Master Theodoric.

buildStages.title: Royal foundation (1348)
buildStages.description: Charles IV lays the foundation stone for the castle on a high rocky promontory.

buildStages.title: Great Tower (1357)
buildStages.description: The massive square Great Tower is built to house the imperial regalia and relics.

buildStages.title: Holy Cross Chapel (1365)
buildStages.description: The chapel inside the Great Tower is consecrated, decorated with semi-precious stones and panel paintings.

buildStages.title: Neo-Gothic restoration (1880s)
buildStages.description: Architect Josef Mocker carries out a thorough neo-Gothic restoration.

interiorNotes.title: Holy Cross Chapel
interiorNotes.description: Walls clad with 2400 polished semi-precious stones beneath 130 panel paintings of saints.

interiorNotes.title: Audience Hall
interiorNotes.description: The Great Hall with a star vault and replicas of the imperial crown jewels.

interiorNotes.title: Marian Tower chapel
interiorNotes.description: Smaller chapel with rare wall paintings of the Apocalypse.

interiorNotes.title: Treasury
interiorNotes.description: Original keep where the Imperial Crown Jewels were kept until 1421.

historicFacts.description: Charles IV founds the castle as a treasury for the Imperial Crown Jewels.
The Hussite Wars force the relics to be moved to Hungary for safekeeping.
After the Battle of White Mountain the castle ceases to be a royal residence.
Declared a National Cultural Monument of Czechoslovakia.

name: Bell Rock Lighthouse
brief: World's oldest sea-washed lighthouse, standing on a treacherous reef in the North Sea 18 km off the east coast of Scotland.
fact: The lighthouse is built of interlocking stone blocks like a stone jigsaw puzzle, and has not been altered structurally in 200 years.

buildStages.title: Survey by Stevenson (1799)
buildStages.description: Robert Stevenson surveys the deadly Inchcape Reef and proposes a granite lighthouse.

buildStages.title: Workforce barracks (1807)
buildStages.description: A wooden barracks is erected on the reef, where masons can only work at low tide.

buildStages.title: Granite tower (1810)
buildStages.description: The interlocking stone tower of pre-cut Aberdeen granite is built up over four years.

buildStages.title: Light kindled (1811)
buildStages.description: The lantern is lit on 1 February 1811 and has shone almost continuously ever since.

interiorNotes.title: Library room
interiorNotes.description: Small wood-panelled room used as a library for the keepers.

interiorNotes.title: Kitchen
interiorNotes.description: Tiny round kitchen with a coal range, halfway up the tower.

interiorNotes.title: Lantern room
interiorNotes.description: Modern automated optic that flashes white every 5 seconds, visible 18 nautical miles.

interiorNotes.title: Helideck
interiorNotes.description: A small helipad attached to the tower allows maintenance crews to land.

historicFacts.description: Lit for the first time on 1 February.
Automated, the last keepers leave the rock.
Bicentenary of the lighthouse, marked by Scottish historic events.
Officially classified as a Category A listed building.

name: Mont-Saint-Michel Abbey
brief: Medieval abbey crowning a tidal island off the Normandy coast, one of France's most recognisable landmarks.
fact: Twice a day the tide turns the island into a true island, cutting off the causeway when the sea sweeps in faster than a person can run.

buildStages.title: First oratory (708)
buildStages.description: Bishop Aubert of Avranches founds a small oratory after a vision of the Archangel Michael.

buildStages.title: Romanesque abbey (11th c.)
buildStages.description: The first stone abbey is built on top of the rock with cellars supporting the upper church.

buildStages.title: Gothic Marvel (1228)
buildStages.description: The three-storey Gothic monastic complex called La Merveille is added on the north side.

buildStages.title: Spire and statue (1897)
buildStages.description: A new spire is built, crowned by a gilded statue of St Michael at 156 metres above sea level.

interiorNotes.title: Abbey church
interiorNotes.description: Romanesque nave with a Gothic choir rebuilt after a 15th-century collapse.

interiorNotes.title: La Merveille
interiorNotes.description: Three Gothic levels of refectory, scriptorium and cloister built into the cliff face.

interiorNotes.title: Cloister
interiorNotes.description: Suspended cloister with double rows of slender pink granite columns offset for trompe-l'oeil effect.

interiorNotes.title: Crypt of the Big Pillars
interiorNotes.description: Massive Romanesque pillars supporting the choir of the church above.

historicFacts.description: Benedictine monks settle on the rock at the request of the Duke of Normandy.
King Louis XI founds the Order of Saint Michael at the abbey.
The French Revolution suppresses the monastery; the abbey becomes a state prison.
Inscribed by UNESCO as a World Heritage Site.

name: Yueyang Tower
brief: Three-story Chinese tower beside Dongting Lake, considered one of the Three Great Towers of southern China for its poetic and historical importance.
fact: Its fame rests on the famous prose-poem 'On Yueyang Tower' written by Fan Zhongyan in 1046, even though he had never visited the tower.

buildStages.title: Han military review terrace (3rd c.)
buildStages.description: General Lu Su builds a terrace to review naval troops on Dongting Lake.

buildStages.title: Tang refurbishment (716)
buildStages.description: Tang dynasty officials rebuild the structure as a proper three-storey tower.

buildStages.title: Song reconstruction (1045)
buildStages.description: Governor Teng Zigjing rebuilds the tower; Fan Zhongyan writes the famous essay.

buildStages.title: Qing rebuild (1880)
buildStages.description: The current wooden tower with characteristic helmet-shaped roof is completed.

interiorNotes.title: Helmet roof
interiorNotes.description: Unique double-eaved roof shaped like an ancient general's helmet, distinctive among Chinese towers.

interiorNotes.title: Wooden columns
interiorNotes.description: Four central columns rise through all three floors without metal nails.

interiorNotes.title: Calligraphy hall
interiorNotes.description: Each floor displays calligraphic copies of Fan Zhongyan's famous essay.

interiorNotes.title: Lake terrace
interiorNotes.description: Open balconies on every floor with views over Dongting Lake.

historicFacts.description: General Lu Su builds the original review terrace on the site.
Fan Zhongyan writes 'On Yueyang Tower', one of the most famous essays in Chinese.
The current Qing dynasty wooden tower is completed.
Designated a Major Historical and Cultural Site by China.

name: Bell Tower of Xi'an
brief: Ming dynasty bell tower at the very centre of the old walled city of Xi'an, signalling daybreak in the imperial city for centuries.
fact: The original bell, the Jingyun Bell of 1384, weighed five tonnes and now lives in the Forest of Stone Steles museum nearby.

buildStages.title: First Ming tower (1384)
buildStages.description: The Hongwu Emperor's first bell tower is built two blocks west of the present site.

buildStages.title: Relocation (1582)
buildStages.description: The tower is dismantled and rebuilt in the present central square as the city grew.

buildStages.title: Reign-era restorations
buildStages.description: Subsequent Qing dynasty repairs preserve the green glazed tile roofs and red columns.

buildStages.title: Pedestrian island (1996)
buildStages.description: The square around the tower is fully pedestrianised and an underpass connects to the metro.

interiorNotes.title: Bell chamber
interiorNotes.description: A replica Tang dynasty bell hangs from the ceiling, struck on ceremonial days.

interiorNotes.title: Painted brackets
interiorNotes.description: Carved and painted dougong wooden brackets support the great eaves.

interiorNotes.title: Exhibition floors
interiorNotes.description: Rotating exhibitions display Tang and Ming bronze artefacts.

interiorNotes.title: Square viewpoint
interiorNotes.description: Open balconies on each floor look out along the four streets of the old city.

historicFacts.description: Originally built two blocks west by order of the Hongwu Emperor.
Dismantled and rebuilt in the present central square.
Listed as a national monument by the People's Republic of China.
Pedestrian square reopens with the tower as its centrepiece.

name: Olavinlinna Castle
brief: 15th-century medieval Nordic castle on a small rocky island in the middle of Lake Saimaa, the world's northernmost surviving stone castle.
fact: Each summer the inner courtyard hosts the Savonlinna Opera Festival, with stagings of Wagner and Verdi performed inside the medieval walls.

buildStages.title: Royal commission (1475)
buildStages.description: Erik Axelsson Tott begins the castle for the Swedish crown to defend the eastern border.

buildStages.title: Three round towers
buildStages.description: Three massive round towers with thick walls are built to take cannon fire.

buildStages.title: Russian conquest (1714-1721)
buildStages.description: The castle is occupied by Russian troops during the Great Northern War.

buildStages.title: Restorations (1961-1975)
buildStages.description: Conservators stabilise the walls and recreate medieval roofs and interiors.

interiorNotes.title: St Erik's tower
interiorNotes.description: The largest round tower contains the chapel and the dining hall.

interiorNotes.title: Bell tower
interiorNotes.description: Houses a small museum on the castle's history and the Orthodox Church in Finland.

interiorNotes.title: Dungeons
interiorNotes.description: Narrow stone cells in the bailey kept Russian and Swedish prisoners of war.

interiorNotes.title: Opera courtyard
interiorNotes.description: The inner courtyard is fitted each summer with a roof and stage for the Savonlinna Opera Festival.

historicFacts.description: Construction begins under Erik Axelsson Tott for the Swedish kingdom.
Captured by Russian troops during the Great Northern War.
Loses its military function and is abandoned by the Russian garrison.
First opera performance staged in the inner courtyard, starting a tradition.

name: Hampton Court Clock Tower
brief: Tudor gatehouse tower at Hampton Court Palace on the Thames, holding the famous Astronomical Clock of Henry VIII.
fact: The clock predates Copernicus's published heliocentric model, so it shows the sun rotating around the Earth — useful for telling the time of high tide on the Thames.

buildStages.title: Wolsey's gatehouse (1515)
buildStages.description: Cardinal Wolsey builds the Tudor red brick gatehouse as the main entrance to his new palace.

buildStages.title: Henry VIII takeover (1529)
buildStages.description: Henry VIII seizes the palace and adds the astronomical clock above the gate.

buildStages.title: Astronomical Clock (1540)
buildStages.description: Nicholas Oursian completes the great clock with three concentric dials.

buildStages.title: Restorations (19th-20th c.)
buildStages.description: Successive restorations preserve the Tudor brickwork and the clock mechanism.

interiorNotes.title: Clock chamber
interiorNotes.description: Original 16th-century iron mechanism still drives the great clock face.

interiorNotes.title: Astronomical face
interiorNotes.description: Three rotating dials show hour, month, lunar phase and time of high tide at London Bridge.

interiorNotes.title: Anne Boleyn's gate
interiorNotes.description: Below the clock the gateway carries Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn's intertwined initials.

interiorNotes.title: Inner courtyard
interiorNotes.description: Clock Court below the gate is the second of the three Tudor courtyards.

historicFacts.description: Cardinal Wolsey begins the gatehouse as the main entrance to Hampton Court.
Nicholas Oursian completes the astronomical clock for Henry VIII.
The clock face is restored to its original Tudor design after Victorian alterations.
Hampton Court Palace receives over 1.7 million visitors per year.
